coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots/g-C3N4/ZnO nanocomposite as efficient visible-light driven photocatalyst for tetracycline total degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Feng
Shi, Weilong
Guan, Weisheng
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
coredata.dc:description:
Tetracycline (TC) in the water environment always brings negative impacts on human health and ecosystem equilibrium. Here we report that the carbon dots (CDs)/g-C3N4/ZnO nanocomposite prepared by a facile impregnation-thermal method exhibits outstanding photocatalytic activities for the total photodegradation of TC. Notably, the 30wt%g-C3N4/ZnO with introducing 4mLCDs solution (500mg/L) photocatalyst displays the best photocatalytic activity in the degradation of TC (100mL, 10mg/L; 100% degradation within 30min) under very weak visible irradiation (λ >420nm, 18mW/cm2). In present system, CDs deposited on the surfaces of g-C3N4/ZnO heterojunction not only increases the visible-light response, but also promotes the efficiency of electron-hole pairs separation due to its electrons transfer property. Experiments further confirm that the superoxide radical (O2
−) and hydroxyl radical (
OH) formed on the surface of the photocatalyst are in charge of the degradation of TC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
g-C3N4
ZnO
Photocatalysis
Tetracycline

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of g-C3N4, ZnO and g-C3N4/ZnO nanocomposites
Synthesis of CDs and CZ30-CDs nanocomposite
Active species capturing and ESR experiments

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of ZnO, g-C3N4, CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 (purple, black, green and orange lines, respectively). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SEM images of (a) ZnO, (b) g-C3N4, (c) CZ30 and (d) CZ30-CDs4, respectively. (e–h) The corresponding EDS elemental mapping images of CZ30-CDs4. (i and j) TEM and HRTEM images of CZ30-CDs4.

(a) Full XPS survey of CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 nanocomposite (orange and green traces, respectively). High-resolution XPS spectra of CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 composite (orange and green traces, respectively): (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) Zn 2p. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of ZnO, g-C3N4, CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 (purple, black, green and orangelines, respectively). Insets: Digital photographs of (I) ZnO, (II) g-C3N4, (III) CZ30 and (IV) CZ30-CDs4 grains. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Photocatalytic activities and (b) the first-kinetic constant k of as-prepared photocatalysts for TC degradation under visible light irradiation (λ>420nm). (c) The recycling runs for the photocatalytic degradation of TC over CZ30-CDs4 as photocatalyst under visible light irradiation. (d) XRD patterns of CZ30-CDs4 before and after photocatalytic degradation of TC.

(a) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distribution curves (inset) of CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 (green and orange lines, respectively). (b) PL spectra of g-C3N4, CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 composites excited at 325nm. (c) Photocurrent density versus time curves of ZnO, g-C3N4, CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 samples under visible light irradiation (purple, black, green and orange traces, respectively). (d) EIS of ZnO, g-C3N4, CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 composites (purple, black, green and orange lines, respectively). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Luminescence decays (485nm excitation, monitored with 550nm narrow band-pass filter) of the CDs with (a) 2,4-dinitrotoluene and (b) DEA, respectively. Inset: Stern-Volmer plots for the quenching of luminescence quantum yields (485nm excitation) of the CDs by (a) 2,4-dinitrotoluene and (b) DEA.

(a) Trapping experiment of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of TC over CZ30-CDs4 under visible light. (b) The ESR spectra of CZ30-CDs4 under dark and visible light (λ>420nm): DMPO-
O2 − in methanol dispersions and DMPO-
OH in aqueous dispersions.
Schematic diagram of photocatalytic degradation of TC with CZ-CDs as photocatalyst under visible light.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots/g-C3N4/ZnO nanocomposite as efficient visible-light driven photocatalyst for tetracycline total degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Subsurface Hydrology and Ecological Effects in Arid Region, Ministry of Education, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Chang’an University, Xi’an 710064, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Subsurface Hydrology and Ecological Effects in Arid Region
School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, 199 Ren’ai Road, Suzhou, 215123 Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Tetracycline (TC) in the water environment always brings negative impacts on human health and ecosystem equilibrium. Here we report that the carbon dots (CDs)/g-C3N4/ZnO nanocomposite prepared by a facile impregnation-thermal method exhibits outstanding photocatalytic activities for the total photodegradation of TC. Notably, the 30wt%g-C3N4/ZnO with introducing 4mLCDs solution (500mg/L) photocatalyst displays the best photocatalytic activity in the degradation of TC (100mL, 10mg/L; 100% degradation within 30min) under very weak visible irradiation (λ >420nm, 18mW/cm2). In present system, CDs deposited on the surfaces of g-C3N4/ZnO heterojunction not only increases the visible-light response, but also promotes the efficiency of electron-hole pairs separation due to its electrons transfer property. Experiments further confirm that the superoxide radical (O2
−) and hydroxyl radical (
OH) formed on the surface of the photocatalyst are in charge of the degradation of TC.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental issues have aroused wide public concern in the modern society. Tetracycline (TC), as the broad-spectrum antibiotic, is usually directly released into the water environment, which brings a negative impact on human health and ecosystem equilibrium [1–3]. Various technologies have been employed to remove and degrade TC, such as adsorption, electrochemistry and photocatalysis [4–6]. Among them, photocatalysis employing semiconductors as photocatalysts has been supposed to be a promising approach to thoroughly remove TC from wastewater owing to its low budget and moderate reaction conditions.

ZnO, as a common semiconductor material, has gained increasing research attention because of its outstanding electrical and optical properties, low cost, high biological safety, environment benign and strong photocatalytic degradation ability of organic pollutants under UV light [7–11]. However, ZnO with wide band gap (around 3.2eV) cannot be excited under visible light (accounting for 43% of solar spectrum), which restricts its practical application in photocatalysis. In order to enhance the visible light photocatalytic performance of ZnO, the construction of heterojunction of ZnO-based semiconductor with suitable band potentials has provided an effctive pathway to improve the photocatalytic activity. Meanwhile, the ZnO-based heterojunctions also exhibit a higher photocatalytic performance in contrast with the single-component photocatalyst such as AgBr/ZnO [12], Ag2CO3/ZnO [13] and ZnS/ZnO [14]. Recently, several literatures have reported that the g-C3N4-hybridized semiconductors show higher photocatalytic activities than any other pristine one, such as g-C3N4/Ag/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and g-C3N4/InVO4/BiVO4 [15,16]. So far, there are several reports on g-C3N4/ZnO heterojunctions, which also shows excellent photocatalytic activities [17–19]. Nevertheless, the binary g-C3N4/ZnO heterojunction systems still have some drawbacks, one of which is the limited region of visible light response [20,21]. To solve the problem, loading small amount noble metal nanoparticles such as Ag as a co-catalyst to semiconductors have been demonstrated to be effective in enhancing visible light absorption, which is attributed to the surface plasmonic resonance (SPR) of noble metal nanoparticles, leading to the separation of electron-hole pairs such as Ag/g-C3N4/ZnO [22]. Although the decoration of noble metal nanoparticles on the binary composites have been developed, the increasing cost makes such ternary heterojunction hardly meet the need of the practical application. Compared to the precious metal nanoparticles, the carbon dots (CDs), as new-type of nanocarbon, have lower cost and better dissolubility in water, which exhibits excellent electron transfer properties and serves as a multifunctional component in photocatalyst design to promote wider spectrum absorption. In our previous work, CDs/Cu2O and CDs/Ag3PO4 compound systems were illustrated to reveal extraordinary photocatalytic properties both in the range of visible and near-infrared (NIR) [23,24]. Simultaneously, CDs/ZnO and CDs/g-C3N4 have been demonstrated to show enhanced photocatalytic activities compared to single phase ZnO or g-C3N4 [25,26]. Regrettably, ternary photocatalysts of CDs decorated g-C3N4/ZnO have not been reported so far. Thus, the aforementioned analysis motivated us to design CDs/g-C3N4/ZnO photocatalyst.

Herein, we presented an impregnation-thermal method for the synthesis of a ternary heterostructured CDs/g-C3N4/ZnO nanocomposite, which exhibits outstanding photocatalytic activities for photodegradation of TC under visible light. Moreover, the 30wt% g-C3N4/ZnO with introducing 4mL CDs solution (500mg/L) photocatalyst shows the best phtocatalytic activity and stability for total degradation of TC (100mL, 10mg/L; 100% degradation within 30min) under very weak visible light irradiation (λ >420nm, 18mW/cm2). The roles of CDs in the CDs/g-C3N4/ZnO nanocomposite and the possible catalytic degradation mechanism were explored as well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of g-C3N4, ZnO and g-C3N4/ZnO nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The g-C3N4 was synthesized by annealing melamine in muffle [27]. Briefly, 10g melamine was put into an open crucible, which was heated to 500°C and kept for 2h. After that, the muffle was heated to 550°C with a ramping rate of 2°Cmin−1 and held for another 2h. After cooling down, the results were ground into yellow powders. For ZnO nanorods, the 10g zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O) was sintered at 580°C for 2h in air atmosphere. After cooling to room temperature, the products were collected and grounded into white powders. Different mass ratios g-C3N4/ZnO (CZ) was synthesized by a chemical deposition method. As 30wt% CZ for example, in a typical procedure, 0.03g of g-C3N4 were mixed in the 100mL of methanol and sonicated for 30min. Then the rod-like ZnO (0.1g) was added into the suspension with continuous stirring for 24h. After volatilization of the methanol, the 30wt% CZ composite was obtained. On this basis, different mass ratios of CZ composite, 10wt%, 30wt% and 50wt% were prepared and labeled as 10wt% CZ, 30wt% CZ and 50wt% CZ, respectively, which were abbreviate to CZ10, CZ30 and CZ50.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CZ30-CDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized by an electrochemical method [28]. Typically, two graphite rods with a separation of 7.5cm were placed into ultrapure water, in which one was the anode, and the other was the counter electrode. Then, the static potentials of 30V were applied two electrodes using a direct current power supply. After a continuous stirring for 120h, a dark yellow solution was formed in the reaction beaker. To obtain the CDs, the solution was filtered using slow-speed quantitative filter papers, and followed by centrifuging at 15,000rpm for 25min. Thereafter the yellow solution was dried to form CDs powder. The powder was weighted and added to ultrapure water to form the CDs solution of 500mg/L. A typical preparation of CZ30-CDs nanocomposite was as follows: 50mg CZ30 powders and 2mL, 4mL and 6mL CDs aqueous solution (500mg/L) were mixed and vigorously stirred for 2h, followed by vaporizing the solvent at 100°C in an oven. The as-obtained powder was annealed at 300°C in air for 1h. Following the same procedure, different mass ratios of CZ30-CDs nanocomposites with 2wt%, 4wt% and 6wt% CDs, respectively, were synthesized. The obtained samples were named as CZ30-CDs2, CZ30-CDs4 and CZ30-CDs6, respectively.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of products was recorded on an X’Pert-ProMPD/max-γAX-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.154178nm). Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was characterized by a Nicolet 360 spectrometer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and energy dispersive X-ray analysis spectroscopy (EDX) were obtained on FEI-quanta 200 with an accelerating voltage of 20kV. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were taken on FEI-Tecnai F20. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out on an ESCALABMK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using MgKα X-ray (hυ =1283.3eV). The UV–vis absorption spectrum was performed on a Lambda 750 (Perkin-Elmer) spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) study was recorded on a Fluorolog-TCSPC Luminescence Spectrometer. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas were characterized by a Micromeritics ASAP-2050 porosimeter. The fluorescence decays of the CDs were measured by a Jobin Yvon Time-correlated Single Photon Counting System (Horib-fm-2015) with an excitation wavelength of 485nm.

Electrochemical analysis was conducted on a CHI 660b workstation. The Pt wire, a calomel electrode, and the samples were served as the counter electrode, the reference electrode, and the working electrode in a three-electrode cell, respectively. The synthesis of working electrodes was in line with the previous report [29]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded by using an alternating voltage of 5mV amplitude in the frequency range of 105–10−2 Hz with the open circuit voltage in 0.5M Na2SO4. For photocurrent time (PT) analysis, the 300W Xe lamp (cutting off the light of λ<420nm) and Na2SO4 (0.5M) solution were empolyed as light source and electrolyte.

Briefly, 50mg photocatalyst was suspended in 100mL aqueous solution of 10mg/L tetracycline (TC). The solution was stirred for 30min in dark to ensure the establishment of adsorption equilibrium. Visible light (18mW/cm2, xenon lamp) was obtained by using cut off filters to remove light of λ<420nm. At certain time intervals, 3mL aliquots were extracted and centrifugated in the reaction process, analyzed on UV–vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active species capturing and ESR experiments
ce:section.ce:para:
Sacrificial agents such as 2-propanol (IPA), disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) were used to probe hydroxyl radicals (
OH), holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (O2
−), respectively. This method was identical with the preceding photocatalytic activity test with the addition of 1mmol of scavenger. In addition, the ESR technique was recorded on an electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (A300-10/12, Bruker) to further used to detect the presence of
OH and O2
− radicals in the photocatalytic reaction system under visible light (λ>420nm). The
OH and O2
− radicals can be captured by the DMPO. Before to determine the hydroxyl radicals (DMPO-
OH) and superoxide radicals (DMPO-O2
−), 10.0mg samples were dissolved in 0.5mL deionized water (DMPO-
OH) or 0.5mL methanol (DMPO-O2
−), and then 45μL DMPO was added with ultrasonic dispersion for 5min.

The as-synthesized of CDs were obtained by a typical electrochemical method [28]. The TEM image of the CDs is shown in Fig. S1a, which is similar to the CDs structure reported in previous work [30]. The size distribution of CDs is displayed in Fig. S1b, which implies that their diameters are in the range of 2–8nm. XRD patterns of ZnO, g-C3N4, CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 are presented in Fig. 1 a. For ZnO, all the diffraction peaks can be indexed to the standard data (JCPDS no. 36-1451). The g-C3N4 shows the obvious diffraction peak at 27.4°, matching well with (002) crystallographic planes of the layered g-C3N4, respectively [31]. With regard to the CZ30 composite, the peaks of both g-C3N4 and ZnO can be checked. Particularly, the XRD pattern of CZ30-CDs4 is similar to that of CZ30 and the characteristic peak for carbon at 26° is too weak to be found, which is assigned to the slight quantity of CDs in ternary nanocomposites [24]. Fig. 1b shows the FT-IR spectra of the above prepared materials. The g-C3N4 exhibits main peaks at 3300cm−1, 1200–1650cm−1 and 809cm−1, which are attributed to the NH, CN heterocycle and s-triazine units stretching vibration bond, respectively [32]. The absorption peaks of ZnO are observed around 3400cm−1, 1650cm−1 and 503cm−1, corresponding to OH, HOH and ZnO stretching vibration, respectively [27]. For CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 samples, the main characterized stretching vibration bands of ZnO and g-C3N4 can be found to verify the successful formation of heterojunction.

The morphologies of as-prepared samples were characterized by SEM. Fig. 2 a shows that ZnO has a rod-like morphology with about 2.5μm in length with average diameter of around 100nm. In addition, the g-C3N4 displays lamellar-like morphology (Fig. 2b). The morphology of the ternary composite of CZ30-CDs4 (Fig. 2d) has no obvious difference compared to that of CZ30 (Fig. 2c). It is clearly seen that ZnO nanorods are deposited on lamellar g-C3N4 to form the intimate heterojunction. And the elemental information of the CZ30-CDs4 photocatalyst was also recorded by the elemental mapping in Fig. 2e–h, where C, N, Zn, and O elements were detected. Additionally, the corresponding EDX analysis with wt% of all elements is given in Fig. S2. However, it is still hardly observed the presence of CDs on the surfaces of CZ owing to the small quantity of CDs. In order to further confirm CDs anchored on the surface of the CZ complex structure, TEM and HRTEM characterization technique were employed. TEM image of CZ30-CDs4 composite in Fig. 2i reveals that ZnO nanorods are deposited on the surface of laminated g-C3N4 and CDs are averagely attached onto the CZ surface. Fig. 2j shows the HRTEM images of the sample corresponding to the rectangle region of the TEM image in Fig. 2i. It is worth mentioning that the interplanar spacing of 0.28nm is clearly observed, which corresponds to (002) crystallographic spacing plane of ZnO [33,34], while the lattice spacing of around 0.32nm corresponds to the (002) spacing of graphitic carbon [35], which is a solid evidence for the formation of CDs modified CZ heterojunction.

The chemical composition and surface electronic states of CZ30 (green trace) and CZ30-CDs4 (orange trace) were recorded by XPS spectra. In Fig. 3 a, the survey spectra of CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 demonstrate that the main elements in the samples are Zn, C, N, O and In. Particular attention is paid to that the element of In, which is from indium substrate. High resolution XPS of C 1s, N 1s and Zn 2p for CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 are given in Fig. 3b–d. As can be seen from Fig. 3b, the C 1s resolution spectrum of CZ30 shows two peaks at binding energies of 284.5 and 288.1eV, respectively. The former banding energy for CZ30 may be associated with the surface adventitious carbon, and the latter at binding energies of 288.1eV can be corresponded to sp2-bonded carbon (NCN) [36]. In the case of CZ30-CDs4, the XPS spectrum of C 1s can be separated into three component peaks with binding energies at about 284.5, 285.3 and 288.1eV. Compared to CZ30, another strong C 1s peak observed at 285.3 can be attributed to graphitic carbon in CZ30-CDs4 nanocomposite, further suggesting the successful formation of CZ-CDs ternary composite [37]. Fig. 3c shows the XPS N 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4, the N 1s spectrum of CZ30 can be resolved into four peaks centered at 398.2, 399.1, 400.4 and 404.4eV, respectively. The peaks at 398.2 and 399.1eV originates from (CNC) and N(C)3 or HN(C)2. The weaker peak with a high binding energy at 400.4eV is attributed to quaternary N bonded to three carbon atoms in the aromatic cycles [38,39]. And the peak centered at 404.4eV corresponds to the π excitations [40,41]. For the CZ30-CDs4 sample, the XPS spectrum of N 1s is divided into four peaks at 397.7, 398.3, 400.1 and 403.7eV. In contrast with CZ30, CZ30-CDs4 shows a slight shift, indicating that there is a certain interaction among g-C3N4, ZnO and CDs. The same signs are also observed in the Zn 2p XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 3d, the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p5/2 binding energies of CZ30-CDs4 (1020.9 and 1044.0eV) are slightly lower than that of CZ30 (1021.3 and 1044.3eV), also existing that the interaction among g-C3N4, ZnO and CDs [42]. In theory, the enhancement of electron concentration results in the decrease of binding energy in virtue of the promoted electron screening effect. Thus, it is reasonable to conclude that lower binding energies shifts are ascribed to the increased electron concentration of CDs decorated CZ heterojunction and elevate the strong interaction between the CZ heterostructure and CDs based on the interfacial charge transfer.

UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnO, g-C3N4, CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 samples are shown in Fig. 4 . It reveals that ZnO (white powders, purple line) shows a band edge at 390nm (3.17eV) and g-C3N4 (yellow powders, black line) exhibits a typical band gap absorption edge at 460nm (2.7eV), which is similar to the previous literatures [43,27]. For the CZ30 (light yellow powders, green line) and CZ30-CDs4 (grey powders, orange line) heterojunction, two obvious absorption bands are found, which are ascribed to the characteristic absorptions of g-C3N4 and ZnO. These results imply that ZnO cannot be activated under visible light, originating from a wide band gap of 3.17eV, but g-C3N4 has the ability of photoabsorption from UV light to visible light. After introducing g-C3N4 into ZnO, it makes the CZ30 possess the visible light response. Furthermore, when the CDs are uniformly depositing on the surface of CZ30, CZ30-CDs4 presents a higher visible light absorption in the range of 460–700nm, which indicates that CDs play an important role in harvesting sunlight.

The photocatalytic activities of the as-sythesized photocatalysts were explored for degradating TC under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5 a, when no catalyst is added in the reactor, TC is hardly degraded in 60min, showing the self-photolysis of TC is negligible. And pure ZnO also has little effect in degradating TC due to the fact that ZnO cannot be excited by visible light irradiation. On the contrary, pure g-C3N4 has good photocatalytic activity at the same conditions. Meanwhile, the appropriate content of g-C3N4 in the CZ composites is obtained. When the percentage of g-C3N4 increases to 30wt%, the CZ30 composite exhibits excellent photocatalytic activity. Then, the CZ30 composite was chosen as a target sample containing different amount of CDs to further investigate the photocatalystic activities of CZ30 composite. It is obviously found that CDs-CZ photocatalysts present higher photocatalytic activities than CZ30 composite, and the CZ30-CDs4 photocatalyst displays the highest photocatalytic activity in the degradation of TC (almost 100% degradation rate within 30min). However, the degradation rate of TC decreases to 90% in 30min when further increasing the amount of CDs to 6mL. The possible reason should be attributed to the shielding effect of carbon-based materials [44]. Fig. S3 reveals the photocatalytic degradation of TC over as-prepared samples followed the pseudo-first-order kinetics, ln(C0/C)= kt, where C is the concentration of the TC at time t, C0 is the initial concentration of the TC solution, and the slope k is the apparent reaction rate constant. The results imply that k values for all the CZ30-CDs4 samples are higher than those of CZ composites and either pure sample. It can be obviously found that CZ30-CDs4 possesses the maximal degradation rate constant of 0.082min−1, which is almost 4 times as large as that of CZ30 (0.021min−1) and 10.25 times for g-C3N4 (0.008min−1), respectively. Meanwhile, the degradation rate constant k (min−1) for the photodegradation of TC over all the prepared samples the k values of the photocatalysts were also summarised in Fig. 5b. For practical applications, the stability of photocatalyst is a crucial issue in the photocatalytic reaction. Consequently, the photocatalytic stability of CZ30-CDs4 was investigated by recycling ten times in the degradation experiments of TC (shown in Fig. 5c). Although the degradation efficiency slightly decreases after ten runs, the catalyst is quite stable during the repeated experiments. Furthermore, Fig. 5d shows the XRD patterns of CZ30-CDs4 photocatalyst before, after the second time and the tenth time of TC decomposition experiments, respectively. It is noted that no obvious structure changes can be found for CZ30-CDs4 before and after photodegradation experiments, which further proves the CZ30-CDs4 is stable enough. Based on the above analysis, we can conclude that the CZ30-CDs4 photocatalyst is an effective and stable photocatalyst under visible light.

To reveal the origin of the high activity of CZ30-CDs4, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were applied to characterize CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 nanocomposites, and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 6 a. Through calculating the results, the BET specific surface area of CZ30-CDs4 (9.56m2 g−1) is much larger than that of CZ30 (6.38m2 g−1). It is reasonable to reveal that a large specific surface area in heterojunction may favor an increase in the number of active sites and enhance its photocatalytic activity. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of g-C3N4, ZnO, CZ30 and CZ30-CDs4 are shown in the Fig. 6b. PL spectra have been widely used to reveal the charge transfer, migration and recombination processes in photocatalysts [36]. The result displays that ZnO shows weak emission peaks in the range of 400–550nm, which corresponds to excitonic PL and results from surface oxygen vacancies and defects of ZnO nanorods [45]. Different from ZnO, a strong emission peak centered at about 460nm was observed in pure g-C3N4, which is in line with the previous report [46]. Addtion ZnO does not alter the spectral position of the peaks, but reduces the relative intensity of PL spectra in the CZ composite. Compared to g-C3N4 (black trace) and CZ (green trace), the fluorescent intensity of the CZ30-CDs4 system (orange trace) decreases remarkably, indicating that the introduction of CDs into the CZ heterojunction make the electron transfer faster in the CZ-CDs system. Moreover, the charge transfer between ZnO and g-C3N4 semiconductors was also approved by photocurrent (PC) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) experiments. In general, higher photocurrents reveal the effective separation of photo-generated charges, leading to higher photocatalytic activities [47]. According to this relationship, we recorded the PC curves of above samples under visible-light illumination in an on-and-off cycle mode. Clearly, in Fig. 6c, the CZ30-CDs4 photocatalyst exhibits the most noticeable photocurrent density in all the samples, which demonstrates the positive role of CDs in further enhancing photocatalytic activity of CZ composite. Meanwhile, the results of EIS measurements shown in Fig. 6d were in accordance with that of PC results. Classically, a smaller arc size in an EIS Nyquist plot indicates a smaller charge-transfer resistance on the electrode surface [48]. The electrode of CZ30-CDs4 exhibits the smallest arc size. Undoubtedly, CZ30-CDs4 is expected the fastest interfacial charge transfer in all the samples.

To further demonstrate the function of CDs in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of CZ composites, the following experiments were further performed. The photoluminescence in CDs could be quenched efficiently by electron acceptor or donor molecules in solution [49]. Fig. 7 a and b exhibits the luminescence decays (485nm excitation, monitored with a 550nm narrow bandpass filter) of CDs, which were quenched by the electron acceptor 2,4-dinitrotoluene (−0.9V vs. NHE) and electron donor N, N-diethylaniline (DEA, 0.88V vs. NHE), with the obtained Stern-Volmer quenching constants (K SV =τF 0 k q) from linear regression of 32.5M−1 and 20.6M−1, respectively. The results imply that CDs are dominant as both electron acceptors and electron donors, which could further improve the photocatalytic ability of CZ composites for target reaction.

To explore the mechanism for the photodegradation of TC over CZ-CDs, the impacts of active species such as O2
−, h+ and
OH in the photocatalytic reaction process were investigated in Fig. 8 a. Obviously, three kinds of scavengers were used to remove the homologous active species for recording the functions of different active species in the degradation of TC under visible light. After adding EDTA-2Na and BQ, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TC decreases to 87.3% and 10.2%, respectively, which indicates that h+ can be negligible in the photocatalytic process, while O2
− is the main active species in the reaction. In addition, when isopropanol is added, the removal rate also has a drop to 42%, which illustrates
OH is the secondary active species. To sum up, the active species that play main roles in the TC degradation is O2
− and
OH. To further verify the existence of
OH and O2
− radicals, the ESR measurement was carried out and shown in Fig. 8b. It can be seen that the signals of DMOP-
OH and DMPO-O2
− can be clearly observed when CZ30-CDs4 suspension is detected under visible light irradiation, which is in good agreement with the results of active species capturing experiment.

In view of the above experiment results, the possible mechanism of the ternary heterostructured CZ-CDs composite with enhanced photocatalytic performance is proposed, which can be explained in Fig. 9 . First of all, ZnO cannot be excited by visible light irradiation [50], so harvesting light is only performed by g-C3N4 (2.7eV) photocatalyst. Since the CB edge potential of g-C3N4 (−1.12eV vs. NHE) is more negative than that of ZnO (−0.5eV vs. NHE) [51], thus the photo-generated electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 injects directly into that of ZnO under visible light irradiation, leaving the holes in the VB of g-C3N4. Due to the fact that the CB edge potential of ZnO (−0.5eV vs. NHE) is more negative than the standard redox potential E (O2/O2
−) (−0.33eV vs. NHE) [52], it is deduced that the partial electrons on the CB of ZnO can be further bonded with dissolved oxygen in water to form O2
− radicals. Simultaneously, when CDs are deposited onto CZ composite, it will play two important roles during the photocatalytic process. On the one hand, CDs acted as both electron acceptors and donors make electrons easily transfer to the surface of CZ30, and the redundant electrons on CZ30 can also be transferred to CDs, which further improves the separation of electron-hole pairs. On the other hand, CDs anchored CZ composite can help the complex system to absorb wider spectrum of visible light, which excites CZ composite to form more electron-hole pairs. Meanwhile, the photogenerated holes in the valance band of g-C3N4 (E VB =+1.40eV vs. NHE) are incapable of oxidizing hydroxyl groups into
OH radicals (E (
OH/OH−)=+1.99eV vs. NHE) [53]. It is noted that the observed
OH radicals are generated from the O2
− radicals by photochemical reaction. Therefore, in the CZ-CDs nanocomposites, O2
− and hole can act as the major oxidation reagents for TC to make it degrade eventually.

The ternary heterostructured CZ-CDs nanocomposites prepared via an impregnation-thermal method show excellent photocatalytic activities for degradation of TC. In addition, the CZ30-CDs4 photocatalyst shows the extraordinary phtocatalytic activity for total degradation of TC (100mL, 10mg/L; 100% degradation within 30min) under very weak visible light irradiate (λ >420nm, 18mW/cm2). The enhanced photocatalytic ability could be ascribed to the CDs deposited on the surface of the binary CZ composite, which not only absorbs wider spectrum of visible light, but also hinders the combination of electron-hole pairs through its excellent property of electron transfer. This work may provide a new insight into the development of CDs decorated binary heterojunctions as highly efficient photocatalysts for degradation of contaminants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (2012CB825803, 2013CB932702), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51422207, 51132006, 51572179, 21471106, 21501126), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20123201110018), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (BK20140310), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2014M560445, 2015T80581), and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2016.09.040.
